Post_Text: A teacher's spouse got cancer. The treatment ran past $500,000. The school had stop-loss insurance for exactly this. The carrier still denied it and sent the bill back to the school. One contract term did that: [LINK]
The school had stop-loss insurance for exactly this claim. The carrier still sent the bill back to the school. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Your stop-loss contract has two numbers on it, like 12/12 or 12/15. Most school leaders have never had them explained. Those two numbers decide whether a catastrophic claim gets paid or bounced back to you. Here is what they mean: [LINK]
You switched stop-loss carriers and saved on premium. A claim incurred last year hasn't finished paying yet. Your old contract closed. Your new one won't touch it. Guess who pays. [LINK]

At renewal, a stop-loss carrier can single out your sickest employee. They assign that one person a much higher deductible than everyone else. It's called a laser, and it lands when you have the least leverage to fight it. How to see it coming: [LINK]
Self-funded with stop-loss? The terms decide whether your safety net catches a catastrophic claim. The premium quote never tells you that. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)
Notes: Post from company page; consider PDF lead-capture version

Post_Text: Specific stop-loss catches the one huge claim. Aggregate catches the year where small claims pile up past budget. A school carrying only one of them is half-covered and usually doesn't know it. Check which one you're missing: [LINK]

"Aggregating specific deductible" gets sold to schools as savings. It lowers your premium by quietly handing you a second layer of risk to pay first. In a heavy claims year it can cost more than the discount ever saved. Read before you sign: [LINK]

One question for whoever handles your school's benefits: If a claim incurred this year pays out after you switch stop-loss carriers, who covers it, your old contract, your new one, or your school? If the answer is a shrug, read this: [LINK]
